---
title: "Visualization"
subtitle: "Create plots with ggplot2 — scatter, line, bar, and box plots."
toc: true
---

All plots in this course use `theme_classic()` and labelled axes via `labs()`.
Run the setup chunk first — it loads ggplot2 and creates the datasets.

```{webr-r}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)

# UV calibration standards
standards <- tibble(
  conc_ug_ml = c(0, 2.5, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0),
  absorbance  = c(0.001, 0.112, 0.224, 0.445, 0.891, 1.782)
)

# Dissolution profile — two batches
dissolution <- tibble(
  time_min      = rep(c(15, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120), 2),
  batch         = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 6),
  pct_dissolved = c(28, 52, 71, 85, 94, 98,
                    22, 45, 65, 80, 91, 96)
)

# Content uniformity — two batches
capsules <- tibble(
  batch     = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 10),
  pct_label = c(99.8, 100.1, 100.3, 99.6, 100.0,
                100.2, 99.9, 100.4, 99.7, 100.1,
                100.5, 100.2, 100.7, 99.9, 100.4,
                101.0, 100.6, 100.9, 100.3, 100.8)
)
```

## Scatter plot

Use `geom_point()` to plot individual observations — ideal for calibration curves.

```{webr-r}
ggplot(standards, aes(x = conc_ug_ml, y = absorbance)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "UV Calibration Curve — Ibuprofen",
    x     = "Concentration (µg/mL)",
    y     = "Absorbance (AU)"
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

Add a regression line with `geom_smooth()`:

```{webr-r}
ggplot(standards, aes(x = conc_ug_ml, y = absorbance)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#3C5E3E") +
  labs(
    title = "UV Calibration Curve — Ibuprofen",
    x     = "Concentration (µg/mL)",
    y     = "Absorbance (AU)"
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

## Line plot

Use `geom_line()` for data over time — ideal for dissolution profiles.
Map `colour` to a grouping variable to show multiple batches.

```{webr-r}
ggplot(dissolution, aes(x = time_min, y = pct_dissolved, colour = batch)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title  = "Dissolution Profile",
    x      = "Time (min)",
    y      = "% Dissolved",
    colour = "Batch"
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

## Box plot

Use `geom_boxplot()` to compare distributions across groups — ideal for batch comparisons.

```{webr-r}
ggplot(capsules, aes(x = batch, y = pct_label)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Content Uniformity by Batch",
    x     = "Batch",
    y     = "% Label Claim"
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

## Bar chart

Use `geom_col()` when you already have the values to plot (e.g. means from `summarise()`).

```{webr-r}
mean_by_batch <- capsules %>%
  group_by(batch) %>%
  summarise(mean_pct = mean(pct_label))

ggplot(mean_by_batch, aes(x = batch, y = mean_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#5F7D61") +
  labs(
    title = "Mean % Label Claim by Batch",
    x     = "Batch",
    y     = "Mean % Label Claim"
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## ggplot2 layer logic
Every plot starts with `ggplot(data, aes(...))` — this sets the data and axes.
Add layers with `+`: `geom_point()`, `geom_line()`, `labs()`, `theme_classic()`.
Order doesn't matter for most layers, but `theme_classic()` always goes last.
:::
